coredata.dc:title: A portable RGB sensing gadget for sensitive detection of Hg2+ using cysteamine-capped QDs as fluorescence probe
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Bin
Ma, Jun
Yang, Tong
Chen, Lin
Gao, Peng Fei
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: A facile one-pot method was proposed to synthesis cysteamine capped CdTe QDs with a high quantum yield 30%. In the synthesis process, nitrogen protection, ultrasonic treatment, and light irradiation were unnecessary. Additionally, potassium tellurite was used instead of Te powder as Te source, avoiding of the time-consuming Te powder dissolution process. The prepared QDs were found to be Hg2+ sensitive. The detection linear range for Hg2+ was from 5 to 300nM, and the detection limit was calculated to be 1nM. The detection mechanism was mainly ascribed to the electron transfer from cysteamine capped QDs to Hg2+, resulting in a fluorescence quenching phenomenon. In addition, we developed a portable RGB sensing gadget with the QDs as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection, and the linear range was from 5 to 200nM. Furthermore, the gadget was applied for Hg2+ detection in tap water sample. The results showed that the average recoveries in real tap water sample were in the range from 97.2% to 115.3%, which indicated that accuracy and precision were satisfactory for practical application requirements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Portable RGB sensing gadget
Cysteamine capped QDs
Hg ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of cysteamine capped CdTe QDs
Portable RGB color sensing gadget design
Analytical performance of cysteamine-capped QDs for Hg2+ detection
Detection of Hg2+ in tap water sample using the potable RGB color sensing gadget

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence emission spectra of CdTe QDs in the presence of Hg2+ at different concentration in BR buffer at pH 7.54 (A), the relationship between F/F 0 and the concentration of Hg ions (B). F and F 0 are the FL intensities of QDs at 564nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+.

The interference study of different metal ions and anions on the fluorescence of cysteamine capped CdTe QDs at pH 7.54 BR buffer. The concentration of Hg2+ ion 150nM, and the concentration of the other metal ions 1.5μM.
Time-resolved fluorescence of QDs and QDs with 50nM Hg2+ in BR buffer at pH 7.54.
Illustration of cysteamine capped CdTe QDs for Hg ion detection.
The schematic and the actual figures of the portable RGB color sensing gadget (A), the standard detection curve using the gadget for Hg2+ detection (B).
Measurement of Hg2+ in tap water sample using the gadget.

head.ce:title: A portable RGB sensing gadget for sensitive detection of Hg2+ using cysteamine-capped QDs as fluorescence probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile one-pot method was proposed to synthesis cysteamine capped CdTe QDs with a high quantum yield 30%. In the synthesis process, nitrogen protection, ultrasonic treatment, and light irradiation were unnecessary. Additionally, potassium tellurite was used instead of Te powder as Te source, avoiding of the time-consuming Te powder dissolution process. The prepared QDs were found to be Hg2+ sensitive. The detection linear range for Hg2+ was from 5 to 300nM, and the detection limit was calculated to be 1nM. The detection mechanism was mainly ascribed to the electron transfer from cysteamine capped QDs to Hg2+, resulting in a fluorescence quenching phenomenon. In addition, we developed a portable RGB sensing gadget with the QDs as fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection, and the linear range was from 5 to 200nM. Furthermore, the gadget was applied for Hg2+ detection in tap water sample. The results showed that the average recoveries in real tap water sample were in the range from 97.2% to 115.3%, which indicated that accuracy and precision were satisfactory for practical application requirements.

ce:section.ce:para: The mercury ion (Hg2+) as one of the most hazardous environmental toxins is ubiquitous in the environment (Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b; Xia and Zhu, 2008). It can accumulate in the vital organs and tissues of animals, which would results in a variety of diseases (Pei et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b). Hence, developing an effective method for the sensitive detection of trace amounts of Hg2+ is quiet imminent (Du et al., 2013; Kim et al., 2012). The World Health Organization Standard for the maximum allowable level of inorganic mercury in drinking water is no more than 6 ppb (30nM) (Du et al., 2013; Su et al., 2014). Hence, various analysis methods have been developed for Hg2+ detection, including atomic absorption spectrometry (Wan et al., 1998), selectively cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry (Geng et al., 2008), inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (Moreton and Delves, 1998), gas chromatography (Duan and Zhan, 2015) and high performance liquid chromatography (Krishna et al., 2006). Although these instrumental analytical methods could give accurate information of the target subsistence, these methods are costly, complex, time-consuming, and the samples need laborious treatment before detection. Therefore, establishing a simple, sensitive, and selective method and exploring a practical available detection gadget or sensor which could be used on-field are extremely significant.

T-T mismatches in DNA sequence could selectively capture Hg2+ to form thymine-Hg2+-thymine base pairs, as the specific interaction between Hg2+ and the N atom of thymine, which verified that there exists high selectivity between Hg2+ and N atom, and could be used to distinguish Hg2+ from other heavy metal ions (Du et al., 2013; Hu et al., 2016; Li et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2010). However, this approach is costing and sophisticated in operation. Some research groups have attempted to synthesize amino-group capped fluorescence nanoparticles, especial semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), attempting to generate the selective affinity between Hg2+ and N atom (Chen et al., 2007; Chen et al., 2007; Ding et al., 2015; Kuang et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2011; Qin et al., 2013; Qing et al., 2006; Radtchenko et al., 2001; Shen et al., 2013a, 2013b; Shen et al., 2013a, 2013b; Shen et al., 2016; Wang and Guo, 2009; Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b; Wu et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2008; Yuan et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2012). Nevertheless, a common problem exists that either the quantum yield (QY) of prepared fluorescence nanoparticles is relatively low or the detection limit could not meet the practical requirement. Although Kuang et al. (2010) has prepared QDs stabilized by cysteamine with QY as high as 46% (the highest so far). However, nitrogen gas protection combining with strict ultrasonic radiation are indispensable, and the synthesis procedure is multi-step, including extraneous NaHTe precursor preparation using Te powder.

In this work, a simple and effective method was proposed to synthesis cysteamine capped CdTe QDs with QY reaching 30% (green fluorescence emission). It is worth mentioning that during the whole synthesis process, nitrogen protection and ultrasonic radiation are unnecessary. In addition, we adopted sodium tellurite as Te source to avoid the hard and time-consuming dissolution of Te powder. The synthesized QDs were used as the fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection with the detection limit (LOD) 1nM. The selectivity test demonstrated that cysteamine-capped QDs were highly selective to Hg2+. In addition, a portable RGB color sensing gadget was developed to realize the real-time and in-situ Hg2+ detection in real tap water sample. It was demonstrated that the gadget showed a satisficing performance with a linear range from 5 to 200nM, which results met the practical on-site requirements.

Cysteamine, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), cadmium acetate dehydrate (Cd(CH3COO)2﹒2H2O), mercury chloride (HgCl2) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All of these reagents were analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure deionized water produced by a Milli-Q system was used throughout this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cysteamine capped CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process was carried out according to the work of Wu et al. (2012) with a major modification. Briefly, 53.3mg (0.2mmol) Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O was dissolved into 50mL deionized water in a stand-up flask. Then 15.5mg (0.2mM) cysteamine was added, and the pH was adjusted from 5.00 to 6.80 with 1M NaOH or 0.3M HCl solution. After stirring for 5min, 8.9mg (0.04mmol) Na2TeO3 (used as Te source) which was dissolved in 50mL deionized water was added into the above solution. Then NaBH4 (5.0mg, 10.0mg, 20.0mg, and 40.0mg) was added into the precursor solution, respectively. After the reactions proceeded for another 5min, the flask was attached to a condenser, and refluxed at 100°C under open-air conditions. Through controlling the reaction time, CdTe QDs with a series of emission spectra were obtained. The obtained QDs were precipitated with ethanol, and the precipitates were separated by centrifugation and redissolved in deionized water. At room-temperature, QY of cysteamine capped CdTe QDs was estimated by comparison with Rhodamine 6G, whose fluorescence QY was assumed to be 95% in ethanol (Duan et al., 2009; Xu et al., 2010).

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on F2500 spectrometer. Absorbance spectra were recorded using an UV–vis spectrophotometer UV3600. TEM (Tecnai G2 F20) and Zetasizer nano (ZEN3600) were utilized to analyze the morphology and surface charge characteristics of cysteamine capped CdTe QDs. The IR spectrum of QDs is collected on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer. The fluorescence lifetime of QDs is measured with a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). Centrifuge (Allegra 64R) was used to purify QDs solution. Vortex (QL-901) was utilized to blend samples.

In the experiments, all the FL detections were under the same conditions: the slot widths of the excitation and emission were both 5nm, and the excitation wavelength was set at 360nm. First, 50μL of QDs was diluted with 100μL BR buffer solution and 750μL deionized water. After that, a volume of 100μL Hg2+ aqueous solution was added into the above mixture solution. The mixed solution was incubated for 5min before detection, and Fluorescence intensities were recorded in the wavelength range of 450–700nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable RGB color sensing gadget design
ce:section.ce:para: Structural design of the portable RGB color sensing gadget is as shown in Fig. 5 A. A mini-sized 5V lithium battery was served as the system power. Four ultraviolet light emitting photodiode (UV-diode) were chosen as the light source to excite the fluorescence signal of the QDs solution in the reaction cell. A RGB sensor at the bottom of the reaction cell, consisting of four arrays of light signal receivers with the related red, green, and blue light passing filters. Hence, the received R, G, B optical signals from the reaction cell could be changed to the corresponding electrical signals (proportional to the optical signal intensity). Through compiling the program of the microcomputer, relationship between the intensity of the corresponding electrical signals and the concentrations of Hg2+ could be established. The final corresponding R, G, B value, and Hg2+ concentration values could be displayed on the LCD screen, simultaneously. The response time of the RGB sensor is 10ms, and all the C language execution time is less than 0.1ms. Hence, it is suggested that the response time of the portable RGB color sensing gadget is less than 1s. The total volume of the gadget is 15cm in length, 10cm in width, and less than 5cm in height, and the weight is less than 0.5kg.

Images of as-prepared cysteamine capped QDs (under daylight and ultraviolet light irradiation) versus reaction time, mass of NaBH4, and pH were investigated (Fig. S1). It could be obviously seen that with increase in the mass of NaBH4, the total fluorescence characteristic shifted to the red, which tendency was also the same as the results induced by pH and reaction time increase. Hence, we conclude that a relatively lower pH, smaller amounts of NaBH4, and shorter reaction time were conductive to the synthesis of cysteamine-capped QDs with green fluorescence emission. Correspondingly, a relatively higher pH, larger amounts of NaBH4, and longer reaction time were prone to the synthesis of cysteamine-capped QDs with red fluorescence emission. Furthermore, under the same ultraviolet irradiation condition, we found that the fluorescence intensity of QDs with similar fluorescence emitting characteristics under synthesis condition (pH 5.60, the 10.0mg of NaBH4) was optimum, and we took QDs solution under this synthesis condition with reaction time 15min as QDs fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection.

The QY of QDs solutions with different fluorescence characteristics under this condition is calibrated to be 30%, 15%, 12%, 11%, 9% as reaction time increasing from 15min to 4h (Fig. S2). QDs showed emission maximum at 564nm, 595nm, 602nm, 612nm, and 621nm on excitation at 360nm (Fig. S3). The morphology of the as-prepared QDs is as shown in Fig. S4. It could be observed that there was obvious size increase versus reaction time, and the average size of as-prepared QDs were calculated to be 2.75 and 3.38nm, respectively (using Nano Measurer). In addition, zeta potential of cysteamine capped QDs was studied. The result shows that the surface charge characteristics of QDs under different reaction time were positive (Fig. S5).

pH of the solution has a great effect of the fluorescence intensity of the QDs (Xia and Zhu, 2009; Dai et al., 2006). The capping agent used in this work is cysteamine which is a primary amine compound in a weak base, and its existing form is different with the change of pH. As a result, the surface state of cysteamine capped QDs and its related performance as a sensor would be significantly different. Hence, we investigated the pH effect on fluorescence quenching efficiency by Hg2+ in BR buffer at pH 7.12, 7.30, 7.54, 7.98, 8.35, 8.76, and 9.20, respectively (Fig. S6). According to the experimental result, the change in pH has little influence on the fluorescence of QDs, but have a relatively larger impact on the interaction between QDs and Hg ion. With 150nM Hg2+ adding into QDs solution in BR buffer, the calculated fluorescence quenching efficiency first increased, and then decreased versus pH of BR buffer. The optimal Hg2+ detection condition was present at pH 7.54. Hence, the pH used in all the afterward experiments was set at 7.54.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Efficiency
=
(
F
0
− F ) /
F
0
where F and
F
0
were the fluorescence intensities of QDs at 564nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of cysteamine-capped QDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 displays the typical fluorescence spectra as functions of the concentration of Hg2+ increasing from 0nM to 1μM. As demonstrated, fluorescence intensity reduced with the increasing of the concentration of Hg2+, and it was nearly completely quenched when the concentration of Hg2+ was 1μM. In addition, there was no obvious optical shift in fluorescence spectra with Hg2+ concentration increase. The detection limit for Hg2+ was calculated to be approximate1 nM. The detection limit of some other methods for the detection of Hg ion are compared and listed in Table S1. It can be seen that our approach a relatively lower detection limit, which could fulfil the actual criterion.

Effects of different cations on the fluorescence of cysteamine-capped QDs were studied (Fig. S7A). It was demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of QDs was sensitive to Hg2+ and Ag+ (10 times of the concentration of Hg2+). With the concentration of Ag+ decreased to be the same as that of Hg2+, the quenching effect of Hg2+ was more obvious. The other cations showed little or negligible effect on fluorescence quenching even their concentration was 10 times higher than that of Hg2+. In addition, Ag+ could cause the spectral redshift, which phenomenon was not observed with the other metal ions, which could also be used to differentiate Hg2+ from Ag+.

The effect of various coexisting cations and anions on the fluorescence of cysteamine-capped QDs was studied by mixing a concentration of 150nM Hg2+ and other interfering ions at concentration of 1.5μM. The solution was mixed thoroughly, and then added to QDs solution. Fluorescence spectra were recorded in the presence of interfering ions, and the changes of fluorescence intensity and spectral peak position (λ) obtained are shown in Fig. 2. The result demonstrated that merely Ag+ (1.5μM) appears to cause some interference, compared with other cations. Still, the red-shift caused by Ag+ could be used to discriminate Hg2+ from Ag+.

To understand the fluorescence quenching nature, fluorescence lifetime measurement was carried out in present and absent of Hg2+ ( Fig. 3). The fluorescence lifetime of QDs solutions decreased from 25.4ns in the absence of Hg2+ ion to 17.1ns in the presence of 50nM Hg2+. Therefore, the quenching was concluded to be dynamic in nature. In the absence of Hg2+, under UV excitation wavelength of 360nm, cysteamine-capped CdTe QDs emit green fluorescence (564nm). In the presence of Hg2+, the fluorescence of QDs is quenched. Capping agent, cysteamine, a bifunctional ligand, could afford thiol groups to strongly bond Cd2+ on the QDs surface by chemical bond with hydrophilic amine groups to achieve water-compatibility of the QDs. According to the investigation in these references (Kim et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2012a, 2012b), the quenching mechanism of Hg2+ on the fluorescence of cysteamine capped QDs might be attributed to three main reasons. The first reason is the electron transfer from QDs to Hg2+ because of the strong affinity of Hg2+ to nitrogen atom (Miyake et al., 2006). Secondly, the substitution of Cd2+ in CdTe QDs by Hg2+ would leads to the formation of alloyed QDs (Cai et al., 2006; Susha et al., 2006). Finally, the band alignment between the core and the surface band structures for fluorescence quenching for that the lower band gap of HgTe than that of CdTe is responsible for this quenching behavior as well (Pendyala and Koteswara, 2009). Hence, the assay principle is descripted as shown in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in tap water sample using the potable RGB color sensing gadget
ce:section.ce:para: The scheme and the actual figures of the portable RGB color sensing gadget are as shown in Fig. 5A. Since the QDs used here was green fluorescence emission, we took the component green value (G) as the output signal versus the concentration of Hg2+. Through establishing the standard detection curve (as shown Fig. 5B), the linear detection range for Hg2+ was from 5 to 200nM. For the purpose of further demonstrating the feasibility of the gadget, it was applied to the determination of Hg2+ concentration in tap water. The water sample was filtered through qualitative filter paper (0.45µm and 0.22µm). The results showed that Hg2+ was not present in these real samples, so we use the standard addition method to perform the average recovery test. Table 1 showed that the average recoveries in real tap water sample was in the range from 97.2% to 115.3%, and that the RSD is lower than 13.6%, which results indicating that accuracy and precision are satisfactory for practical application.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized cysteamine-capped CdTe QDs with a high QY using a quiet facile approach without nitrogen gas protection and Te powder. Through controlling the synthesis time, QDs with different fluorescence emitting spectra were obtained. In addition, we found that these QDs could be used for the sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection, and the LOD was calculated to be 1nM. The main fluorescence quenching mechanism was ascribed to be the bonding effect of Hg2+ to the surface of QDs and the energy transfer from QDs to Hg2+. Furthermore, a portable, sensitive RGB sensing device was developed for the in-situ and real-time Hg2+ detection in tap water sample. The detection linear range was from 5nM to 200nM. It is worth mentioning that the application field and extendibility of the gadget is wide as long as there presents an intensity change in color related signal. The gadget is smart in volume, and could be used independent of electrical powder supplying system. Hence, it is suggested that the gadget has a potential to be used in the practical on-field situation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21535006), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2016M602631, 2016M600713), and the Central Universities under Grant numbers SWU115023.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.05.032.
